albums.title: Discovery
albums.reason: With Digital Love, One More Time, and Superheroes all sitting in your all-time top tracks, this is the record they come from — and it's probably been a while since you let the whole thing play through.

albums.title: In Rainbows
albums.reason: Reckoner is your third most-played track of all time, and it lives here — this album has that rare quality of feeling both emotionally heavy and strangely comforting.

albums.title: The Dark Side of the Moon
albums.reason: Pink Floyd sit deep in your all-time top artists at nearly 1200 plays, and this is the one that rewards a full uninterrupted listen more than almost any record ever made.

albums.title: Melody A.M.
albums.reason: Röyksopp are firmly in your all-time top five with over 1000 plays — this debut is their most purely beautiful record, and it's the kind of thing that sounds different every time you return to it.

albums.title: Moon Safari
albums.reason: Air sit at over 1000 all-time plays for you, and Moon Safari is the record that defined their whole aesthetic — unhurried, cinematic, and completely timeless.

albums.title: Leftism
albums.reason: Release the Pressure is your fourth most-played track ever and it lives here — Leftism is one of the great electronic albums and it probably hasn't had a full play from you in years.

albums.title: Dubnobasswithmyheadman
albums.reason: Born Slippy is in your all-time top 20 tracks and Underworld sit in your all-time top artists — this debut is rawer and stranger than their later work, and absolutely worth revisiting.

albums.title: Is This It
albums.reason: The Strokes have over 650 all-time plays from you, and this album still sounds like nothing else — tight, nonchalant, and almost shockingly good start to finish.

albums.title: Immunity
albums.reason: You love the space between Daft Punk's electronic architecture and Radiohead's emotional weight — Immunity lives exactly there, building from fragile piano into overwhelming club catharsis.

albums.title: Black Sands
albums.reason: Your liked track Kiara is a strong signal here — Black Sands is the album that put Bonobo on the map, and it has that same warm, jazz-tinged downtempo depth across every track.

albums.title: Galactic Melt
albums.reason: If you love the glossy electronic sheen of Daft Punk and the melancholy streak in Röyksopp, Com Truise's debut sits right in that zone — hazy, nostalgic, and oddly moving.

albums.title: II
albums.reason: Given your love of Leftfield, Underworld, and James Blake, Moderat's second album is a natural fit — electronic music with genuine emotional heft and production that rewards close listening.

albums.title: Music Has the Right to Children
albums.reason: Your deep investment in Daft Punk, Orbital, and Röyksopp points straight at this — Boards of Canada's debut is one of the most beloved records in electronic music and genuinely unlike anything else.

albums.title: Kill for Love
albums.reason: You love the cinematic cool of Air and the melancholy in James Blake — Kill for Love is a sprawling, late-night record that feels like a film score for a life slightly out of focus.

albums.title: Supermodified
albums.reason: With Orbital, Justice, and Leftfield in your all-time top artists, Amon Tobin's Supermodified is an obvious adjacent move — intricate, jazz-sourced, and full of the kind of detail you clearly love.

albums.title: Dive
albums.reason: Given how much you love the atmospheric end of Air and Röyksopp, Tycho's Dive is a record you'd likely adore — shimmering guitars and warm synthesis that feels perpetually golden-hour.

albums.title: The Last Resort
albums.reason: Your love of Leftfield, Underworld, and the darker textures of Radiohead makes this a compelling match — Trentemøller's debut is a cult classic of European electronic music, brooding and beautifully produced.

albums.title: Promises
albums.reason: Your recent deep listening to Nils Frahm, Hania Rani, and the Oscar Peterson Trio points directly at this — a single 46-minute piece co-created with jazz legend Pharoah Sanders, it's one of the most extraordinary records of recent years.

albums.title: Black Focus
albums.reason: Your recent love of Oscar Peterson and the soul-jazz warmth of Bill Withers and Charles Bradley maps perfectly onto this — Black Focus is UK jazz at its most propulsive and joyful.

albums.title: Knee-Deep in the North Sea
albums.reason: You've been spending serious time with Hania Rani and Nils Frahm this year, and Portico Quartet occupy a beautiful space between acoustic jazz and ambient music that sits right in that current listening arc.

albums.title: Portraits
albums.reason: With Weval, DJ Koze, and Nightmares on Wax featuring heavily in your recent listening, Maribou State's debut is a perfect fit — organic, soulful electronic music with a genuine emotional pulse.

albums.title: At the Pershing: But Not for Me
albums.reason: Your Oscar Peterson Trio deep-dive this year and the Night Train album sitting at the top of your recent plays makes this a natural step — Ahmad Jamal's live masterpiece is every bit as swinging and intimate.

albums.title: v2.0
albums.reason: Given your love of Nils Frahm, Martin Kohlstedt, and Hania Rani this year, GoGo Penguin's breakthrough album is an exciting adjacent move — piano trio jazz with a minimalist, almost electronic sensibility.

albums.title: Tawk Tomahawk
albums.reason: Your recent time with Sault, Charles Bradley, and Bill Withers shows a real pull toward soulful, emotionally direct music — Hiatus Kaiyote are the most inventive band in that neighbourhood and this debut is where they announced themselves.

albums.title: Every Day
albums.reason: Your listening this year — from 9 Lazy 9 and Nightmares on Wax through to Brambles and Apparat — traces a clear love of music that sits between jazz and electronic; Every Day is one of the great albums at exactly that intersection.

albums.title: The Lagos Music Salon
albums.reason: Your consistent return to Sault and the rich soul vein in your recent listening — Charles Bradley, Bill Withers — opens the door here; Somi is a stunning vocalist making jazz-rooted music with deep African and soul influences.

albums.title: Solipsism
albums.reason: You've played Joep Beving 17 times in the last month and 15 times just this week — Solipsism is his debut and the record that started it all, quieter and more searching than Liminal.

albums.title: Waltz for Debby
albums.reason: You've been deep in Bill Evans this month with New Jazz Conceptions, and Waltz for Debby is the other side of that — live, loose, and one of the most beloved piano jazz records ever recorded.

albums.title: Geography
albums.reason: You've been playing Full Circle heavily this past month — Geography is Tom Misch's breakthrough album and has that same warm, unhurried guitar-led sound that you're clearly in the mood for right now.

albums.title: Smokers Delight
albums.reason: Nightmares on Wax are featuring consistently in both your monthly and recent listening — Smokers Delight is their most hypnotic and sensuous record, perfect companion to the jazzy, late-night mood you're in right now.

albums.title: Starfruit
albums.reason: You've had Voyager on rotation this month — Starfruit is Moonchild at their most lush and jazz-forward, and it fits the soft, soulful, after-hours mood your recent track history is painting.

albums.title: Solo
albums.reason: With Nils Frahm in both your monthly and weekly top artists right now, Solo is the most stripped-back and personal thing he's recorded — just him, a small upright piano, and complete silence around it.

albums.title: We Get Requests
albums.reason: You've played the Oscar Peterson Trio 36 times this month — Night Train is already in your rotation, and We Get Requests is its perfect companion: unhurried, masterful, and deeply satisfying.

albums.title: Colour of Sound
albums.reason: Talkin Bout Life has been in your recent rotation and Aroop Roy keeps appearing in your recent data — Colour of Sound expands that same warm, globe-trotting jazz-soul sound across a full album.

albums.title: Reflections
albums.reason: You've had Felix Rösch's Piano album and Berceuse in your recent and current rotation — Reflections captures the same quiet, meditative quality and fits perfectly into the still, introspective mood you're clearly in this week.

songs.title: Harder, Better, Faster, Stronger
songs.reason: You've got six Daft Punk tracks in your all-time top 20 but this one isn't among them — which means it's probably been quietly sitting in the vault for a while, waiting for the right moment.

songs.title: Pyramid Song
songs.reason: Reckoner is your third most-played track ever, which tells you something about how you connect with Radiohead at their most hypnotic — Pyramid Song is that same quality taken somewhere even deeper.

songs.title: Remind Me
songs.reason: Röyksopp sit firmly in your all-time top five with over 1000 plays — Remind Me is one of their most quietly affecting tracks and the kind of thing that sounds different depending on your mood.

songs.title: The Wilhelm Scream
songs.reason: Postpone is your most-played track of all time and CMYK is also in your top 20 — The Wilhelm Scream is James Blake at his most emotionally direct and it belongs in the same conversation.

songs.title: Halcyon + On + On
songs.reason: Orbital sit in your all-time top artists with 777 plays and this is arguably their most beloved track — a piece of electronic music so delicately beautiful it almost doesn't seem like it should exist.

songs.title: Kashmir
songs.reason: Led Zeppelin have nearly 1000 all-time plays from you, and Kashmir is the track that earns its eight minutes more fully than almost anything in rock music — worth returning to properly.

songs.title: Runaway
songs.reason: Kanye West sits in your all-time top 20 artists, and Runaway is one of those tracks that demands to be heard in full — nine minutes of piano, orchestration, and ego that somehow works completely.

songs.title: Citizen Erased
songs.reason: Muse have 712 all-time plays from you and this is their most ambitious track — seven minutes of shifting dynamics that shows the full range of what they can do at their best.

songs.title: She Moves She
songs.reason: Your all-time love of Boards of Canada-adjacent electronics and the atmospheric end of Röyksopp points here — this track from Rounds is one of the most quietly joyful pieces of electronic music of its era.

songs.title: Open Eye Signal
songs.reason: Given your deep love of Justice, Daft Punk, and the harder end of your electronic taste, Open Eye Signal is a landmark — a track that builds with the patience and payoff of the best music you love.

songs.title: Roygbiv
songs.reason: Your love of the melodic, emotionally warm end of electronic music — Daft Punk's Veridis Quo, Röyksopp, Air — makes Roygbiv an almost certain hit; it's two minutes of pure, aching beauty.

songs.title: Bad Kingdom
songs.reason: The emotional weight you clearly love in Radiohead and James Blake finds a natural electronic equivalent here — Bad Kingdom is Moderat at their most affecting and one of the great crossover electronic tracks.

songs.title: Sun
songs.reason: Your love of the melodic, layered end of electronic music — Daft Punk, Air, Röyksopp — opens the door to Caribou, and Sun is their most immediately beautiful track, kaleidoscopic and oddly moving.

songs.title: Shadow
songs.reason: The cinematic melancholy you love in Air and Radiohead finds an unexpected meeting point in Chromatics — Shadow is hypnotic, slow, and the kind of track that seems to get under your skin without you noticing.

songs.title: Moan
songs.reason: Given your love of Leftfield, Underworld, and the atmospheric end of electronic music, Moan is a standout — sparse, brooding, and with one of the most distinctive builds in European electronica.

songs.title: Awake
songs.reason: The warm, unhurried atmosphere you love in Air and the melodic richness of Röyksopp maps perfectly onto Awake — it's one of those tracks that physically changes the light in the room when it plays.

songs.title: LesAlpx
songs.reason: Your recent listening to DJ Koze, Four Tet, and Weval shows you're in a sophisticated electronic headspace right now, and LesAlpx is Floating Points at his most thrillingly forward-moving — jazz energy routed through club hardware.

songs.title: Strings of Light
songs.reason: Your recent Oscar Peterson Trio obsession and the soul-jazz warmth of your 12-month listening makes this a natural fit — Strings of Light is propulsive, meditative, and impossible to sit still to.

songs.title: Hopopono
songs.reason: Given your recent immersion in Nils Frahm and Martin Kohlstedt, GoGo Penguin's Hopopono is an exciting next step — piano trio jazz with an almost electronic momentum that feels genuinely fresh.

songs.title: Nakamarra
songs.reason: Your recent soul arc — Bill Withers, Charles Bradley, Sault — combined with your love of intricate electronic production points straight at Nakamarra, one of the most strikingly original soul tracks of the last decade.

songs.title: Poinciana
songs.reason: Your Oscar Peterson deep dive this year is the clearest possible pointer to this — Poinciana is one of the most famous piano jazz performances ever recorded, hypnotic in a way that genuinely transcends genre.

songs.title: To Build a Home
songs.reason: Your year-long listening — Brambles, Apparat, Hania Rani — shows a clear pull toward music that's patient and emotionally vast; To Build a Home is one of those tracks that exists in that same rare space.

songs.title: Tongue
songs.reason: Your recent rotation of Weval, DJ Koze, and Four Tet points clearly at Maribou State, and Tongue is the track that best captures what makes them special — warm, soulful, and rhythmically alive.

songs.title: Alien
songs.reason: Your recent love of Sault's soulful collectivism and the emotional directness of Charles Bradley opens the door here — Alien is Somi at her most arresting, a track that stops you in your tracks.

songs.title: Sleeping Lotus
songs.reason: You've played Joep Beving more than anyone else this week — Sleeping Lotus is from Solipsism and is perhaps his single most quietly overwhelming piece, perfect for the introspective mood your listening history is showing.

songs.title: Peace Piece
songs.reason: Given where you are right now — deep in contemplative piano music from Joep Beving to Felix Rösch — Peace Piece is a natural fit; Evans playing alone, completely still, for nearly seven extraordinary minutes.

songs.title: Movie
songs.reason: You've been playing Full Circle this month and Movie is Tom Misch at his most unhurried and warm — a perfect track for the relaxed, jazz-inflected mood your current listening is describing.

songs.title: The Truth
songs.reason: Voyager has been in your recent rotation and The Truth is Moonchild at their most intimate — a quietly devastating vocal performance over brushed drums and Rhodes that fits your current after-hours mood exactly.

songs.title: Says
songs.reason: Nils Frahm is in your top artists for both the last month and last seven days — Says is the track that most captures the slow, transcendent build that makes his music so absorbing right now.

songs.title: Autumn Leaves
songs.reason: You've been living with the Oscar Peterson Trio for the whole month — their version of Autumn Leaves from We Get Requests is one of the most beautifully measured things they ever recorded, and it's the right track for right now.

songs.title: South Wind
songs.reason: scruscru is your second most-played artist this week and South Wind is a standout from South Wind, Clear Sky — that same beautiful intersection of jazz feeling and delicate house groove that you've clearly been craving.

songs.title: Our Prayer / The Path to Your Heart
songs.reason: Folamour has been appearing in both your monthly and recent track history — this track captures the devotional, emotionally generous side of his sound that fits perfectly into the warm, soulful mood you're in right now.

djSets.setName: HMWL Podcast 430: Niconé & David Hasert (Kompakt / Get Physical / HMWL)
Daphni - FOLD, London. Nov 22, 2025. 7hr DJ set
Klausmarie b2b Alec Troniq - RAVIN' RAVIN' 2026 @ Sektor Evolution
BENT THE KNEE TO HER VOL. 1
